questions.prompt: Grammar — Subjunctive: "The committee insists that every delegate ___ present at the opening session."
questions.explanation: After insist + that, the mandative subjunctive requires the base form (infinitive) for all subjects, including third-person plural: every delegate be present. "is" is the indicative form (no subjunctive). "being" is the gerund or present participle (wrong form after that). "was" is the past indicative, not the subjunctive base form.

questions.prompt: Vocabulary — Idiom: "After years of rivalry, the two companies finally ___ and decided to merge."

options.text: let the cat out of the bag

questions.explanation: To bury the hatchet means to end a conflict and make peace — perfect for ending a rivalry before a merger. "burn bridges" means to destroy relationships permanently (the opposite — you can't merge after burning bridges). "let the cat out of the bag" means to reveal a secret. "bite the bullet" means to endure a painful situation stoically — not about reconciliation.

questions.prompt: Grammar — Unreal past: "I wish I ___ to the meeting yesterday — it sounds like it was very useful."
questions.explanation: I wish about a past event (yesterday) uses the past perfect: I wish I had gone. "went" (past simple after wish) refers to a present/future wish (I wish I went to work by bike — habitual), not a missed past event. "go" is the base form (no tense). "would go" after wish expresses a strong desire about another person's future behaviour, not a personal regret.

questions.prompt: Usage — Confusable words: "The study ___ that higher caffeine intake may lead to improved focus."
questions.explanation: A study that suggests something without stating it directly implies it — the speaker/author implies, the reader infers. "infers" reverses the direction (the reader would infer from the study, but the study itself implies). "supposes" means to assume or hypothesise — not what a study does when it suggests an association. "affects" means to influence — the study doesn't influence here; it communicates.

questions.prompt: Usage — Ellipsis: "Tom said he would call back, and so ___ Lisa."
questions.explanation: The ellipsis replaces said she would call back: the auxiliary did substitutes the past action. In so + auxiliary inversion (so did Lisa), the auxiliary did echoes the past reference of said. "said" would need an object (so said Lisa is archaic/poetic and the ellipsis is incomplete). "would" keeps the future sense but drops the past context of said. "does" is present tense and doesn't match the past narrative.
